data.title: With the August deadline fast approaching, the Clarity bill faces even greater challenges in passing.
data.description:
Author: Little Bear Biscuit; Source: Bitpush

With the summer recess approaching, Senate Majority Leader John Thune has pledged to push for a full Senate vote around August 7th, officially putting the Digital Asset Market Clarity Act into its final stretch.

On July 22nd, Senate Republicans released a fully updated draft of over 600 pages, merging and unifying the previous work of the Banking Committee and the Agriculture Committee. This bill aims to establish a long-term regulatory framework for the crypto capital markets, decentralized finance, and stablecoins, attempting to end the industry's long-standing period of regulatory ambiguity.

However, the legislative process is not smooth. Intense debate surrounding a key "ethics clause" in the draft is escalating, with neither side willing to back down. Some observers worry that if the stalemate continues, this highly anticipated bill may be embroiled in the increasingly close midterm elections, ultimately missing its window of opportunity.

On one hand, there's the industry's yearning for regulatory certainty; on the other, there are the legislators' political calculations—whether this bill can pass before the recess remains uncertain. The core controversy: Ethics Clause and the Trump Family's Interests. The most notable change in this updated text is the encryption ethics restrictions negotiated between Lummis and Moreno and the White House without Democratic approval. The White House previously praised it as "the most comprehensive and wide-ranging ethics clause in history" and confirmed that Trump was willing to sign a bill containing this clause.

1. Scope of Restrictions and Potential Loopholes

The plan prohibits the president, vice president, members of Congress, federal judges, and their spouses or employees from receiving compensation through the issuance or sponsorship of digital assets (such as tokens) during their term of office. Restricted officials must sell their cryptocurrency and shares in crypto companies, or place them in uncontrolled blind trusts; transactions exceeding $1,000 must trigger mandatory disclosure, and the U.S. Government Accountability Office (GAO) will investigate any remaining loopholes.

However, critics and Democrats point out obvious targeted loopholes and circumventions in the provision:

  • Neglects Immediate Family Members: The ban only applies to the official, their spouse, and employees, and does not restrict the official's children or other family members from issuing or sponsoring digital assets, meaning that the president's immediate family members could still profit from crypto projects.

    The draft bill, while prohibiting officials from using their names, likenesses, images, or official titles to promote digital assets, includes an exemption—if the digital asset was issued before the official took office, the issuer can continue to use their likeness. This exemption is clearly for the Trump family. Trump issued his personal meme coin, $TRUMP, just three days before his inauguration. Forbes estimates that Trump will earn approximately $635 million through $TRUMP alone by 2025, and approximately $800 million through World Liberty Financial, in which he holds a 40% stake (which also issued tokens). Ethics disclosures show that Trump raked in as much as $1.4 billion from crypto projects in the first year of his second term. Senator Warren's office issued a statement saying the new draft bill is "full of loopholes" and will effectively allow Trump to continue profiting through World Liberty Financial. Sunset Clause: This ethics clause has a defined expiration date and will automatically expire on January 20, 2029 (the day the next president is sworn in). 2. Enforcement Authority: A Deadly Point of Contention Between the Two Parties Besides the clause itself, the biggest point of contention is who will enforce it: The Republican and White House proposal: Complete civil enforcement authority will be transferred to the Department of Justice (DOJ) and the Attorney General, with a clear prohibition on state attorneys general intervening in enforcement. For exchanges that illegally list prohibited tokens, the DOJ can impose a fine of up to $250,000 per day for each violation; for the violating official, the DOJ will forfeit illegal gains plus a $500,000 fine or 10% of the illegal gains. Democrats strongly oppose the bill: Senate Democrats believe that having the DOJ solely responsible for enforcement, especially with former presidential lawyer Todd Blanche nominated as Attorney General, is tantamount to building castles in the air. Maryland Democratic Senator Angela Alsobrooks (who had previously supported removing the bill from committee) publicly criticized the bill at the Semafor meeting, calling the unilateral transfer of enforcement power to the Justice Department "absurd, unserious, and outrageous," and emphasizing that given the current state of the DOJ, it should not be relied upon entirely. She and six other Senate Democrats issued a joint statement on Wednesday, clearly pointing out serious deficiencies in the current draft's ethical and enforcement provisions.

    Underlying Market Rules: Preserving Core Consensus

    Compared to the moral controversies at the forefront, the bill's other core chapters concerning the technology and market structure of the crypto industry remain largely unchanged, and the overall industry assessment is positive:

    Developer and Self-Custodial Protection: The Blockchain Regulatory Certainty Act (BRCA) continues the version from the Banking Committee in May, explicitly excluding non-custodial developers and underlying infrastructure providers from the definition of "money transmitters"; it also retains the Lummis-Grassley Amendment's criminal liability for knowingly assisting illegal transactions and establishes self-custodial rights through the Keep Your Tokens Act.

    Stablecoin Yield Compromise: It adopts the Tillis-Alsobrooks compromise, prohibiting the payment of interest on idle payment-type stablecoin balances, but allowing compliant rewards based on network activity.

    Combating Illicit Finance and Bankruptcy Liquidation: The newly added enforcement chapter establishes cybercrime centers targeting North Korea and Iran, provides funding for local crypto investigations, and requires stablecoin issuers to comply with legal freezing and seizure orders. Regarding the FTX collapse, the bill explicitly stipulates that customer assets must be treated as separate customer property during bankruptcy proceedings and cannot be included in the liquidation assets of the bankruptcy trustee. Industry organizations such as Grayscale stated that the bill will create long-term legal certainty for developers, issuers, and investors, and is a key infrastructure for promoting the large-scale adoption of public blockchains, stablecoins, and tokenized assets (RWA).

    Market Forecasting Data

    As the legislative window narrows, market participants' expectations for the bill's passage show significant "divergence and rationality." Traders generally assess the "Senate vote" and "final signing into law" stages separately:

    Senate vote probability: On Kalshi, the contract trading price regarding "whether there will be a Senate vote before the August recess" is around 72%, but due to a trading volume of only $31,000, liquidity is relatively shallow.

    Final Passage Probability: On Polymarket, the probability of the bill being signed into law by 2026 is approximately 41% (volume $2.4 million); on Kalshi, a deeper money market, the probability is around 42% (volume $3.6 million). Neither of these two deep money markets has priced the final passage rate above 50%, making the market's claim that "the passage rate is already over 50%" a very optimistic expectation. Future Outlook and Time Window: Currently, there are less than three weeks left before the Senate's summer recess: Phase One (late July – early August): Republicans need to reach the 60 bipartisan threshold in the Senate to pass the bill in the full Senate. Negotiations will be extremely difficult due to the need for the support of key Democrats like Kirsten Gillibrand, and the efforts of external left-wing groups (such as Indivisible and Demand Progress) to obstruct Democrats from accepting a "weak moral agreement." Phase Two (September): If the Senate successfully passes the bill, the House of Representatives will take over the consideration of revised versions after reconvening in September. Phase Three (Executive and Regulatory): After the president signs it into law, the CFTC (Commodity Futures Trading Commission) and SEC (Securities and Exchange Commission) will formally begin drafting specific rules. If the bill ultimately fails to achieve a breakthrough before the recess, the crypto industry will have to grope its way forward in vague guidance without long-term legal protection.

data.title: AI costs are soaring: Meta raises another $12 billion, with borrowing costs rising significantly.
data.description:
Author: Zhao Ying, Wall Street Insights

As tech giants continue their investment spree in AI infrastructure, the bond market is pricing in higher prices for this cash-burning race. Meta's latest $12 billion data center financing shows that investors' risk premium for AI-related debt is rising significantly, with borrowing costs much higher than the previous deal nine months ago.

On Friday, according to the Financial Times, citing sources familiar with the matter, the yield on this bond, led by BlackRock and used for a nearly 1-gigawatt data center project in El Paso, Texas, has exceeded 7% in the initial discussion phase. Some investors are demanding a risk premium that is about 0.4 percentage points higher than Meta's "Hyperion" data center deal completed last October. The sources added that pricing discussions are still in the early stages, and the issuance could officially begin as early as next Monday, with final terms potentially subject to change.

Rising borrowing costs reflect the bond market's growing wariness of the risks associated with AI financing.

Meanwhile, AI concept stocks have recently experienced a large-scale sell-off, and stock investors' doubts about the sustainability of this sector's boom continue to rise, creating a resonance between the sentiments of the two markets. Rising borrowing costs are prompting the market to reprice AI risks. The cost pressures of this financing have substantial implications for the bond market. A credit investor specializing in investment-grade debt stated: "When you issue hundreds of billions of dollars in bonds, even a 0.1 percentage point increase in costs translates to tens of millions of dollars more in interest payments annually, which has a very significant impact in high-rated markets." For reference, Meta's previous "Hyperion" project bond issuance, which completed a record-breaking $27 billion issuance last October, saw its bonds linked to the project, issued through a special purpose vehicle called "Beignet Investor," fall to approximately 96 cents face value this Thursday. Sources familiar with the matter pointed out that the increased borrowing costs directly reflect lenders' cautious attitude towards the company's continued expansion of its AI exposure—a caution that is intensifying following the recent borrowing spree among tech giants. Special Purpose Vehicle Structure: Tech Companies Keep Their Balance Sheets "Clean" This financing continues the structural design of Meta's previous transaction. The bonds will be issued through a special purpose vehicle called "Sopaipilla Investor"—a name derived from a popular South American fried pastry, similar to the previous "Beignet Investor" named after a Louisiana dessert. Sopaipilla will hold 80% of the Texas project, with Meta holding the remaining 20%. S&P analyst Viviane Gosselin stated that this transaction is "almost a carbon copy of the previous one." Borrowing through project entities rather than corporate entities has become a mainstream method for tech companies to raise funds in the AI ​​arms race while maintaining a clean balance sheet. Last month, Anthropic also completed a $35 billion financing round through a scheme backed by GPU leases and a Broadcom guarantee. The structure is robust, but certain risk clauses exist. Regarding the bond structure, Sopaipilla's bonds will mature in 2048, secured by Meta's 20-year lease payments starting in 2028. Meta has four renewal options every four years, with hefty penalties for early exit, providing strong protection for lenders. Furthermore, Meta assumes construction risk, bearing costs exceeding 105% of the initial budget. However, this structure also has limitations: the bonds are not directly pledged as tangible assets. S&P stated in its report that if the project suffers a serious unforeseen event resulting in a delay exceeding 18 months, Meta can terminate the lease without incurring any penalty. Regarding ratings, S&P rated the bonds A+, one notch below Meta's corporate rating of AA-. Gosselin stated, "From our perspective, this is a very robust structure." Fitch and KBRA rated the transaction AA-, consistent with Meta's corporate rating.

data.title: Selling tools or selling results? AI companies are heading towards two completely different futures.
data.description:
Author: Variant; Translator: Deep Tide TechFlow

AI companies are branching into two species: one sells tools to law firms, and the other becomes a law firm itself. This framework article by Variant breaks down why some industries should focus on enhancement and others on automation—the key lies in responsibility, validation costs, and relationship dependencies. For entrepreneurs and investors, this is a practical standard for judging the ceiling of AI companies.

The first wave of AI companies mainly focused on workflow enhancement. Many of them have become the most successful businesses in the history of enterprise SaaS. By 2026, model capabilities will reach a tipping point, and startups will begin to change their approach. They will no longer sell tools to existing players, but will directly disrupt them through automation.

The first wave of AI companies mainly focused on workflow enhancement. Many of them have become the most successful businesses in the history of enterprise SaaS. By 2026, model capabilities will reach a tipping point, and startups will begin to change their strategies. They will no longer sell tools to existing players, but will directly disrupt them through automation.

Many of these companies don't look like SaaS; they're more like Software as a Service (SaaS) itself. The opposition between augmentation and automation aligns with Chris Dixon's classic framework: strong technology versus weak technology. Weak technology is skeuomorphic, replicating old practices with new technologies, usually just more efficient. Strong technology, on the other hand, "starts from first principles, fully utilizing existing resources to design what technology should be." Take legal services as an example. Harvey sells monthly SaaS subscriptions to law firms, while its competitor, Crosby, is itself a law firm. AI does legal work, and human lawyers only intervene where it's most needed: building and maintaining client relationships, validating work, and taking responsibility when things go wrong. Companies selling augmentation tools don't want to disrupt clients and organizational structures; those are their bread and butter. Companies selling automation, however, are suggesting the world needs a major overhaul. Like service companies, AI companies doing automation tend to sell results rather than seats, tokens, or subscriptions. Chris mentions in the article that both strong and weak technologies can succeed, often just on different timelines; both paths have produced and will continue to produce significant results. The question is which path better suits the specific job, market, and the evolving state of technological capabilities. At Variant, we use a simple framework to determine which workflows are suitable for automation and which are suitable for enhancement. Following this, we can see what kind of vertical companies might emerge and where venture capital-scale opportunities will arise. We look at three dimensions: Responsibility: How dangerous and expensive is it when AI makes mistakes? Validation: How quickly and cheaply can users verify the quality of work? Relationships: How much does the experience depend on interpersonal interaction? Tasks with high responsibility, high verification costs, and high dependencies are suitable for enhancement. This framework is evident in the comparison between Crosby and Harvey. Large companies' internal legal departments meet all three criteria of this framework: high responsibility, high verification costs (many open tasks), and high dependencies. Enhancement tools like Harvey or Claude Cowork make sense in this scenario. Conversely, Crosby focuses on more routine, high-frequency legal services such as commercial contracts, which typically have lower responsibility, are easier to verify, and have weaker dependencies, serving startups. We learn similar rules when building autonomous systems on public blockchains: smart contracts can only act on verifiable things. Anything unverifiable must be handled off-chain by humans, usually through governance. Crypto tokens follow similar rules: they are extremely good at rewarding verifiable quantity (hash power, staking, liquidity), but extremely bad at rewarding subjective quality. This is why crypto has successfully launched financial markets but hasn't yet taken on Airbnb or Uber. The lessons learned here are increasingly applicable to determining where truly autonomous systems can scale. Today, true automation is more limited in terms of application scope and reachable market size. Startups focusing on automated workflows are often best suited for narrow beachhead markets, selling to startups or niche markets less resistant to a change of pace. Starting in a narrow wedge market is a way to avoid the "kill zone" of large AI labs, which focus on horizontal expansion to enhance a larger existing market. Over time, we believe automation solutions will gradually erode markets that rely more on relationships and higher accountability, as model capabilities expand, validation costs decrease, human-AI interaction crosses the uncanny valley, and cultural acceptance increases. In that scenario, the first-mover advantage gained from beachhead markets could compound, allowing startups to grow alongside the market itself. The landscape and long-term appeal of automation and enhancement will continue to diverge across different industries. Take education as an example. Alpha School is a vertically integrated AI-powered private school that replaces teachers with "facilitators" to facilitate a largely automated learning process. While we believe AI-assisted education will be extremely important, we don't think over-automation and verticalization will expand beyond niche markets because parents worry about teacher-student relationships and schools worry about liability. Much of our education system is rigid (and mostly public) and unlikely to change anytime soon. Even top private institutions face the innovator's dilemma. Therefore, solutions focused on enhancement seem more likely to produce big results, but will also face competition from existing players. Recruiting, on the other hand, points in the opposite direction: we favor automation. Harvey to Crosby in this vertical is: Juicebox sells candidate sourcing software to recruiters, while Prism is the recruiter itself—give it a job brief, it delivers candidates ready for interviews, and only charges when someone signs a contract. Its key stages are easily verifiable (response rate, interview performance, contract rate), and liability is relatively low. Recruiting is highly relationship-dependent, but it's more upstream (primarily finding candidates and converting them into interviews). This is also a function the company often outsources, so the first customers were startups: they preferred to buy results rather than build a team. The largest companies have internal recruiters and choose augmentation tools because they are less willing to automate themselves. The target customers are startups; this is a fine wedge, allowing automation solutions to be embedded in data-rich processes, improving over time and growing with the market. There are two types of AI companies: augmenters and automators. Both extend human autonomy, but in different ways. Augmenters give people more leverage within existing organizations: more knowledge, more output, more initiative. Automators broaden access: expert services that were once only affordable for enterprises become accessible to everyone, freeing up time for more ambitious endeavors. The latest opportunity is not just selling software, but becoming the service itself.

data.title: Singapore's sovereign wealth fund: Chinese AI is lowering global AI costs and accelerating its adoption.
data.description:
Author: Bao Yilong, Wall Street Insights

Singapore's sovereign wealth fund GIC stated that the rise of Chinese AI models will significantly reduce the cost of applying AI technology globally.

On July 23, Bryan Yeo, Chief Investment Officer of GIC, stated in an interview with the Financial Times that the emergence of large-scale Chinese language models such as DeepSeek and Kimi will drive down costs, thereby accelerating the widespread application of AI in more enterprises and industries.

Bryan Yeo characterized this trend as a positive contribution to the global AI ecosystem. He emphasized:

Use cases will only grow exponentially.

OpenAI and Anthropic have both achieved valuations approaching $1 trillion this year, their logic built on the ability to continuously develop top-tier closed-source models.

Analysts believe that if low-cost Chinese open-source models become widespread, the rationale for the aforementioned high valuations will be questioned. GIC is betting on Anthropic and is bullish on Chinese AI. GIC has placed AI at the core of its investment strategy, investing billions of dollars in the field over the past few years. In February of this year, GIC led a $30 billion funding round for Anthropic. If the company behind Claude goes public as planned this year, GIC is expected to reap substantial returns. However, when pressed on whether Chinese open-source models would pose a threat to closed, cutting-edge US models like Anthropic and OpenAI, Bryan Yeo deliberately avoided giving a direct answer. Meanwhile, GIC expressed a positive view on the growth prospects of Chinese AI companies but remained cautious about investing in startups. Bryan Yeo pointed out that GIC needs to "more rigorously examine the actual capabilities of these startups in terms of R&D investment and model iteration." Annual returns hit a recent low, GIC adjusts performance evaluation framework. GIC released its latest annual report on Friday, ending in March of this year. The report shows that the annualized return adjusted for inflation over the past two decades was 3.4%, the lowest level since 2020. GIC does not disclose single-year returns or the total size of its investment portfolio, but is widely considered one of the world's largest sovereign wealth funds. Regarding its performance evaluation framework, GIC adjusted its reporting methodology this year. Previously, GIC used a reference portfolio constructed based on the Singapore government's risk appetite as a benchmark, but stated that this reference portfolio should not be used as a benchmark for evaluating its returns. Starting next year, GIC will switch to publishing performance data for a new strategic portfolio based on government risk appetite and long-term return expectations, which will serve as its official performance benchmark. In terms of asset allocation trends, GIC is continuously increasing its investment in the Americas market. Since 2024, GIC's allocation to the Americas has increased from 44% to 53%, while its allocation to the Asia-Pacific region has decreased from 28% to 22%. Furthermore, GIC stated its plan to deploy $30 billion to hedge funds over the next three years. Over the past decade, GIC's investment in hedge funds has tripled, but specific exposure levels have not yet been disclosed.

data.title: Visa and Lianlian completed the first real B2B smart agent transaction in Greater China.
data.description:
Author: Zheng Jiayi, Wall Street Insights

On July 24, Visa and Lianlian Digital jointly announced that they had completed their first real B2B intelligent agent transaction executed by Lianlian's AI intelligent agent, LoopXPay.

This is also the first publicly disclosed transaction of this type in Greater China.

This transaction served a small business seller;

In terms of process, LoopXPay completed supplier sourcing, solution comparison, order placement, and payment within a single workflow, operating entirely within the enterprise's pre-set expenditure control and approval parameters.

LoopXPay's role goes beyond simply recommending suppliers; it directly connects procurement decisions with payment execution—enterprises only need to pre-set budgets, permissions, and approval conditions, and the intelligent agent can complete daily procurement within the authorized scope, while the enterprise retains necessary supervision and control.

LoopXPay's role extends beyond simply recommending suppliers; it directly connects procurement decisions with payment execution—enterprises only need to set budgets, permissions, and approval conditions in advance, and the intelligent agent can complete daily procurement within the authorized scope, while the enterprise retains necessary supervision and control.

For payment institutions, whether AI can complete payments is only the first step. After an AI agent initiates a transaction in the name of a company, new problems need to be addressed in the payment chain: who authorizes it, the scope of authorization, the trustworthiness of the transaction counterparty, and how to trace anomalies. As an important part of the cooperation between the two parties, LoopXPay has registered in Visa's Agentic Directory. This directory aims to help merchants and companies identify verified AI agents and, in conjunction with the Visa Trusted Agent Protocol, provide identity verification, transparency, and expenditure control mechanisms for agent transactions. This is equivalent to adding a layer of "identity registration" for AI agents entering the payment network: previously, payment institutions mainly verified cardholders, merchants, and devices; after an agent participates in a transaction, the verification scope is further expanded to include the software entity that performs tasks on behalf of the company. However, this mechanism is still in its early implementation stage. The information released by both parties has not yet disclosed the transaction amount, product category, specific payment method, or the manual confirmation steps required from demand identification to final payment. Therefore, the outside world cannot currently assess its level of automation and commercial replication costs. Especially in B2B scenarios, procurement often involves multiple stages such as contract terms, goods acceptance, invoice processing, and cross-border compliance. A successful single transaction proves that the intelligent agent can enter the procurement and payment process, but it does not equate to its ability to handle complex enterprise procurement. Visa and Lianlian revealed that their next plan is to expand their cooperation to areas such as procurement, digital advertising optimization, and B2B platform payments. Lianlian Digital stated that its capabilities for building intelligent agent businesses will cover identity authentication, transaction authorization, smart payments, and global fund settlement. From the perspective of the evolution of the payment industry, AI agents previously focused more on customer service, marketing, and operations; now, they are beginning to acquire the ability to execute orders and allocate funds. The questions that payment infrastructure needs to answer have shifted from "how to enable AI to make payments" to "how to verify who it is, how much it can spend, and who is responsible for its actions." When AI begins to spend money on behalf of businesses, identity, authorization, and accountability mechanisms will become more important than the payment action itself.

data.title: Nvidia and AMD clash head-on – who will define the new standard for intelligent agent CPUs?
data.description:
Author: Rita; Source: Deep Tide

Tide Guide

Nvidia and AMD are vying for the right to define standards in the $170 billion intelligent agent CPU market.

Amid the intelligent agent AI wave, server CPU architecture routes are diverging. Nvidia advocates for faster cores, believing that single-core performance determines the system's upper limit. AMD, on the other hand, focuses on more cores, emphasizing that concurrent throughput is key.

Nvidia released its Vera CPU architecture last week, featuring 88 custom ARM cores, 1.2TB/s memory bandwidth, and a single-chip computing die design. The logic is that intelligent agent AI requires repeated interaction between the CPU and GPU, with each cycle relying on the previous step; therefore, single-core performance directly determines the overall response speed.

AMD will respond at AI Day on Thursday, and the market expects it to focus on a "more cores" approach. Currently, the EPYC 9965 already achieves 2.4 times the rack-level throughput of Vera in a 100kW deployment scenario. Bank of America's assessment of this technology race is clear: whoever defines the next generation of industry benchmarks will win. Two technology paths, two design philosophies. Nvidia's Vera CPU differs from traditional "core-stacking" server processors. While 88 cores aren't outstanding in the server CPU field, Nvidia prioritizes "maximum single-threaded performance." Vera boasts 1.2TB/s memory bandwidth and 3.4TB/s on-chip interconnect bandwidth, with all design elements geared towards improving single-core efficiency. Nvidia's logic is that agent AI differs from one-time large-scale parallel computing; it's more about repeated interactions and loops between the CPU and GPU. Each step—tool invocation, code execution, retrieval, orchestration, etc.—depends on the previous step. Insufficient single-core performance will slow down the entire agent's response speed, causing GPU waiting and consequently reducing the overall utilization of the AI ​​factory. AMD's design philosophy is quite different. AMD believes that production-grade AI is closer to a distributed software platform, with components such as databases, APIs, vector storage, orchestration engines, caches, and middleware running in parallel. In this scenario, the system bottleneck lies in the number of concurrent workflows that can be handled within a fixed power budget. The EPYC 9965's rack-level throughput in a 100kW deployment is 2.4 times that of Vera, and the next-generation EPYC 6 is expected to increase this to 3.3 times.

x86 vs. ARM: The Hidden Battle in the Software Ecosystem

Besides the battle over core count—"faster and more"—there's a more hidden but equally important front: instruction set selection, the ecosystem battle between x86 and ARM.

Nvidia Vera is based on the ARM architecture. Nvidia believes that as long as the microarchitecture is good enough, instruction set differences are not important. Can ARM handle AI workloads? The answer is yes. Can ARM run enterprise software? If its performance surpasses x86 across the board, the software ecosystem will migrate accordingly.

AMD and Intel clearly hold different views. Their argument is direct: intelligent agent AI is extending from model inference to enterprise-level workflows. Databases, middleware, security platforms, enterprise applications, and other scenarios have all been optimized based on the x86 architecture for the past few decades.

The feasibility of ARM replacing x86 cannot be proven solely by a few AI test results. With AI Day approaching, AMD has a window to respond. AMD's AI 2026 Day, held on Thursday, will be the first public response to this debate over the future direction. Bank of America expects that AMD will not simply compete on speed through benchmark scores, as that would fall into Nvidia's "single-core performance" evaluation framework. AMD needs to redefine its competitive dimensions, shifting from "single-core performance" to "the ability to support intelligent agents in real-world production environments." Bank of America believes this is the core of the debate. The key to victory is not a comparison of technological superiority, but rather which industry-defined standard is ultimately accepted. The insight of Bank of America's report lies in its transformation of a CPU technology debate into a struggle for industry "standard-setting power." The computing power requirements of agent-based AI differ from those of traditional AI training. Traditional training relies heavily on massive parallelism, while agent-based AI employs a hybrid of serial loops and concurrent scheduling. Which architecture is superior depends on the definition of the evaluation criteria. If the industry uses "single agent response latency" as the core metric, Nvidia's approach is more reasonable. If the industry uses "single rack agent capacity" as the core metric, AMD has the advantage. Thursday's AMD AI Day will be a crucial juncture in this debate. However, Bank of America implicitly believes that the debate will not be resolved in the short term. The ultimate winner may not be the one with the higher benchmark score, but rather the vendor that can push the industry to accept its defined measurement standards. For investors, the investment value of this debate lies not in judging whether a path is right or wrong, but in understanding the strategic bets of each company. Nvidia is betting on the extreme latency sensitivity of AI agents, while AMD is betting on the core requirement of production environments being concurrency density. Both paths have the potential to succeed, ultimately depending on the actual evolution of AI applications. Bank of America has given both companies a buy rating, with a target price of $350 for Nvidia and $620 for AMD. This indicates that Bank of America believes this competition is not a zero-sum game; both companies can achieve growth through differentiated approaches, just with different growth paths. Disclaimer: This article is a compilation and interpretation of a third-party brokerage research report (Bank of America Securities, July 22, 2026) by Chaoxiang Research. The ratings, target prices, earnings forecasts, and related judgments cited in this article represent the views of the brokerage analyst and are solely those of their institution. They do not represent the views of Chaoxiang Research and do not constitute any investment advice. Investing involves risk; independent decision-making is essential. This article should not be used as a basis for buying or selling any securities.

data.title: Tsinghua University prodigy + rock drummer – this young man from Chaoshan is making Silicon Valley uneasy.
data.description:
Author: seed.eth; Source: Bitpush

Late at night on July 16, 2026, when Yang Zhilin's Kimi K3 model went live, no one anticipated what would happen next.

On the first day, it topped the Arena AI Front-End Code Arena with 1679 points, surpassing Claude and GPT. Some overseas called it the "DeepSeek 2.0 moment."

On the second day, Musk wrote "Impressive" on social media, immediately announcing that his new 2 trillion parameter model "may surpass Kimi," to which Dark Side of the Moon responded: "Welcome Musk to the '2 Trillion+ Club'."

On the third day, Kimi's user requests exceeded expectations, approaching the cluster's capacity limit, and the team issued a late-night announcement suspending new user subscriptions.

On the fourth day, White House officials publicly accused The Dark Side of the Moon of "stealing technology" and "circumventing chip export controls." On the fifth day, the Nasdaq opened down 1.8% due to factors such as the release of K3, and the Philadelphia Semiconductor Index fell 5.2%. On the sixth day, Bloomberg reported that K3 is considered likely to narrow the AI ​​gap between China and the US, with some scholars pointing out that the gap could be reduced to two to three months. On the seventh day, Hu Xijin published an article reminding Yang Zhilin, "Don't go to the US for now." In seven days, a 90s-born man from Shantou threw Silicon Valley, the White House, and Wall Street into disarray. Born in 1992 into an ordinary family in Shantou, Guangdong, Yang Zhilin's growth trajectory was extraordinary from the very beginning. As a teenager, he had two passions: rock music and coding. Attending Shantou Jinshan High School, he was selected for an informatics Olympiad training program despite having no prior programming experience. While most of his classmates started coding in junior high, he was a latecomer. However, just one year later, he won first prize in the Guangdong division of the National Olympiad in Informatics for Youth, securing his place at Tsinghua University. But he was determined to prove himself more than just a "guaranteed admission student." He passed the entrance exam again through the independent admissions process and then decided to take the national college entrance examination (Gaokao) as a regular student—scoring 667 points, becoming the top science student in Shantou. His three admissions to Tsinghua University made him a local legend.

Yang Zhilin (center), the top science student in Shantou City's 2011 college entrance examination, received a free air ticket from Air China. (Image source: Hualong Chaoshan Network)

After entering Tsinghua University, he was assigned to Thermal Engineering—commonly known as "boiler operation." In his sophomore year, he made a decision that puzzled others: to switch majors to Computer Science. The reason was very "rock and roll": he liked it. And this liking came from a novel by Haruki Murakami—the character of a programmer who writes code late at night to bring technology to life filled him with longing.

... Changing majors meant having to retake all the first-year programming courses. However, he ultimately graduated first in his class, with 90% of his major courses scoring above 95. At the same time, he formed the rock band Splay at Tsinghua University, serving as drummer and songwriter. The band's name comes from the data structure "Splay Tree"—a clever pun; in 2014, they won the "Best Original Song Award" at the Tsinghua University Song Contest.

(Top GPA, countless papers)

Years later, when Yang Zhilin recalled his biggest regret from his undergraduate years, he said: "My band didn't win the original song competition; we only got an original song award."

Entrepreneurship is written in the genes of Chaoshan people

（Image source: People Magazine）

Employees of The Dark Side of the Moon say that the company has no departmental walls, and in a sense, no departments at all. Yang Zhilin's signature only has four words: Direct Communication. If a task requires the cooperation of others to complete, just contact the person directly; no approval, no coordination, no meetings are needed. More than ten employees have said that they are more used to dealing with AI because AI is more reliable and simpler. Everyone is very lively in group chats, but they are all quiet when they meet in person. One employee used one word to describe this atmosphere: shy. Most brands want a story, but Kimi's employees remind visitors: don't write "Pink Floyd," and don't mention the piano outside the office. Returning to the 2011 academic record of Shantou Jinshan Middle School, Yang Zhilin wrote to his younger schoolmates: "Believe that things will fall into place naturally. You can do anything you set your mind to." Looking back more than a decade later, this statement is not only a commentary on his own life but also seems like a prophecy for an entire generation. They didn't take shortcuts or detours; they did what needed to be done, and the world heard them.

data.title: Musk: AI may wipe out humanity, but I'll still ride it and enjoy the journey.
data.description:
Author: Zhao Ying; Source: Wall Street News

Faced with the unstoppable AI frenzy, Elon Musk has presented an aggressive timeline and pragmatic regulatory solutions: He predicts AI will surpass human intelligence within five years, and leading companies in the industry should immediately establish a "mutual review" mechanism for their models to mitigate safety risks.

Recently, Tesla CEO Elon Musk gave an exclusive interview to Zanny Minton Beddoes, editor-in-chief of The Economist. In this interview, Musk not only provided his latest assessment of the timeline and potential risks of artificial intelligence (AI) surpassing human capabilities, but also elaborated for the first time on his regulatory vision for leading AI model development companies, and frankly discussed the power struggles among key figures at Silicon Valley's AI giants.

AI will surpass human intelligence within 5 years, with a 10% to 20% probability of ending humanity, forcing AI to "board the ship"

The market is currently highly focused on the timeline for AGI's implementation. Musk has given an extremely clear and aggressive prediction: "In about 5 years, the intelligence level of artificial intelligence may surpass that of humans."

Accompanying this explosion of intelligence is the loss of control. When asked whether humans will still be in control 10 years from now, Musk bluntly stated that it is "unlikely."

He used a brutal yet vivid biological analogy to explain this logic: "If the difference in intelligence between artificial intelligence and humans is far greater than the difference in intelligence between artificial intelligence and chimpanzees, then it's hard to imagine chimpanzees taking the lead. We are actually just evolved versions of chimpanzees; not long ago, we were still eating bananas in the jungle." Previously, Musk had repeatedly warned that AI could lead to the end of humanity. In this interview, when asked if the probability of killer robots causing the end of humanity was still between 10% and 20%, Musk did not deny the risk, but he chose a fatalistic optimism. He used a rocket launch as a highly vivid analogy. When asked, "If there's a 10% to 20% chance your rocket will explode and kill you, would you still board it?" Musk gave an affirmative answer: "Yes. The only thing we can do is minimize the probability of a bad outcome." He further explained the practical logic behind his inevitability to "board": "The development of artificial intelligence and robotics seems unstoppable; even if I wanted to stop it, I couldn't." He believes that since it cannot be stopped, the best option is to be involved in order to influence its direction, "the most likely outcome being huge benefits for everyone." Excluding extreme factors such as global thermonuclear war, Musk predicts that around 2036, humanity is most likely to usher in an era of "extreme material abundance, where everyone can have everything they want." He also called for the establishment of an AI "mutual inspection mechanism": using business competition to maintain honesty. As the arms race among AI giants such as OpenAI, Google, and Anthropic on underlying large-scale models intensifies, how to effectively regulate them has become a focus of market attention. In this interview, Musk proposed a very practical solution: having competitors review each other's models. Musk revealed that he had a long conversation with DeepMind founder Demis Hassabis, who believed the most urgent approach was to "have leading AI companies hold meetings or exchanges at least regularly to discuss any security-related issues, such as weekly or bi-weekly." Musk pointed out that government officials lack deep technical backgrounds and find it difficult to judge whether a cutting-edge model should be released. Therefore, security should be ensured through the commercial competition between giants: "Give competitors just a week or two to review a new model, and they can find problems, thus incentivizing all competitors to be honest." The core logic of this mechanism is that "competitive companies can recognize that certain things may constitute security risks, so they will not hesitate to point out that the release of competitors' models should be postponed." Government intervention is only necessary if companies refuse to address risks. Musk emphasized that this mechanism must be established immediately, as the industry's suggestion of "establishing it within six months" is far too long, given that AI breakthroughs are occurring multiple times a day. He criticized OpenAI for deviating from its original purpose, but expressed a willingness to set aside personal differences for the sake of global interests. When discussing the barriers to cooperation in the AI ​​industry, Musk made no secret of his dissatisfaction with OpenAI's current CEO, Sam Altman. This blunt statement revealed the root cause of the lack of trust among top AI labs. "I don't like Sam Altman because OpenAI was originally a company aiming to achieve open-source artificial intelligence, a company that should belong to the world. However, somehow, it has become an $800 billion profit-making company using closed-source technology," Musk stated bluntly, adding that this completely contradicted the original intention of his donation. At the same time, Musk also revealed some inside information about Anthropic (one of the highest-valued AI startups, founded by former OpenAI core members): "Dalio (Anthropic CEO) also disliked Sam Altman. In fact, the reason the Anthropic team left OpenAI was because they didn't trust Sam Altman; otherwise, they would still be working at OpenAI now." Despite the strong personal grudges among the giants, Musk showed a conciliatory attitude at the end of the interview. He described the Anthropic team as "principled people who care about the future of the world." To promote the establishment of an AI security mutual inspection mechanism, Musk appealed: "For the benefit of the world, let's put aside our personal differences." (Transcript) Musk believes that in about 5 years, the intelligence level of artificial intelligence may surpass that of humans. Five years is roughly the predicted timeframe. So, aside from being human, artificial intelligence can indeed do better than humans.

Host

So, what will life be like in the future?

Musk

The most likely scenario is that we will enter an era of extreme material abundance, where everyone can have everything they want. This may sound absurd, but we are currently living in 2026. Let's see what our situation will be like in 2036.

Of course, there are factors that could hinder the realization of this goal, such as a large-scale global thermonuclear war. However, assuming that World War III does not occur, I think we will enter an era of material abundance. So, this can perhaps be considered a sign of optimism and excitement about the future.

Host: Are you sure that in 10 years, humanity will no longer be in control? Musk: I think that's unlikely. If the difference in intelligence between AI and humans is far greater than the difference between AI and chimpanzees, it's hard to imagine chimpanzees taking the lead. We're actually just an evolved version of chimpanzees; not long ago, we were eating bananas in the jungle, and we still are. Host: Do you think there's a 10% to 20% chance that having killer robots will lead to the end of humanity? You seem quite relaxed about it now. Have you changed your mind? Musk: I still believe there are risks associated with AI and robots, and these risks are not zero. However, I have to take an optimistic view. Regarding the development of artificial intelligence and robots, sometimes I even think that even if there were a stop button, we shouldn't press it—because the most likely outcome is that it will bring enormous benefits to everyone. I realize that now everyone seems to believe that this development is inevitable. Let's hope for the best and keep going. The development of artificial intelligence and robots seems unstoppable; even if I wanted to stop it, I couldn't.

Host

Do you think there's still a 10% to 20% chance that killer robots will lead to the end of humanity?

Musk

That's a really bad situation. In the long run, we will all eventually die. According to the Standard Model of physics, the end of the universe will be a long and tedious process—like a glass of thin, gray liquid gradually cooling down, the so-called "cosmic heat death." So, if cosmic heat death is indeed the final outcome of our reality, then this is all just part of the journey.

Host

If you thought there was a 10% to 20% chance your rocket would explode and kill you, would you still get on it?

Musk

Yes. The only thing we can do is minimize the probability of a bad outcome. I've always been reluctant to get involved in artificial intelligence or related fields. OpenAI existed as a counterweight to Google because Google practically monopolized the development of AI at the time. Now, Anthropic has become the leader in AI. These moves have actually accelerated the development of AI, which wasn't my intention. It all seems to point to further development of AI. So I thought, okay, you can be sad about it, or join in—and you obviously have already joined.

Host

If you can't beat them, join them.

It's understood that other leaders in model development are also working to mitigate the risks. Just last week, Demis Hassabis proposed establishing a public-private partnership regulatory body. Do you think you should be more actively involved in this? Are there any measures we can take to control these risks? Musk: Yes, my suggestion to Demis was that I spoke with him for several hours before we published his article. We believe the most urgent thing is to have the leading AI companies hold meetings or exchanges at least regularly to discuss any safety-related issues, every few weeks. Why haven't you done this yet? I mean, not all of you like each other; you're all competing, all wanting to be number one. Musk: However, I think this competition can encourage honesty. It's indeed difficult for government officials without deep technical backgrounds or who aren't at the forefront of AI development to judge whether a model should be released. However, for competitors, giving them just a week or two to review a new model allows them to identify problems, thus incentivizing all competitors to be honest. This way, model creators can check each other's work. If there's anything that worries them, they can tell the government, which can then determine if there's a problem that needs addressing—and if the company hasn't taken any steps to address the risk, then it's time for the government to intervene and take action. Action should be taken immediately. People have been telling me that such a system must be built within the next six months because the pace of model improvement is accelerating. Six months is indeed a long time; it should be shortened. There are so many breakthroughs in AI right now, sometimes several a day, and there were many last week. So, you need to get together now and get this sorted out. That sounds like what you're telling me. Musk: Yes, I would suggest holding some informal meetings at least regularly, like weekly or bi-weekly. This ensures that there's a week or two to get advance access to the content. Musk: That's why I think this incentive works—competitive companies are aware that something might pose a security risk, so they won't hesitate to point out that a competitor's model should be delayed. Moderator: You don't really like each other, you lack trust, and you often attack each other on social media. So, do you think you can treat people you don't like that? There are definitely personal grudges between these leaders. Elon Musk: I dislike Sam Altman because OpenAI was originally an open-source AI company, meant to belong to the world. However, somehow it became an $800 billion profit-making company using closed-source technology. I feel this completely contradicts the purpose of my initial donation, which is what I'm very unhappy about. Also, Ray Dalio dislikes Sam Altman. In fact, the reason the Anthropic team left OpenAI was because they didn't trust Sam Altman; otherwise, they would still be working at OpenAI now. Elon Musk: I think Ray Dalio is a very principled person. He cares about all sorts of things and the future of the world. So far, everyone I've met at Anthropic has been like that; no one has made me feel they have malicious intent—they all seem to be acting out of goodwill. The road to hell is often paved with good intentions, and while a few may act out of goodwill, we cannot be complacent. However, if a discussion is truly necessary, then let's discuss it. For the sake of the world, let's set aside our personal differences.

data.title: ChatGPT Health Is Now Available Across All OpenAI Plans With Apple Health And Medical Records Integration
data.description:
OpenAI Expands ChatGPT Health Across The US As Safety Questions Grow

OpenAI has opened ChatGPT Health to every logged-in user aged 18 and above in the United States, making the feature available across its Free, Go, Plus and Pro plans.

The wider rollout allows ChatGPT to use connected health information during everyday conversations, bringing medical context into topics that extend beyond dedicated health chats.

The expansion arrives as millions of people continue turning to ChatGPT for health-related questions every week, while OpenAI continues to caution that its chatbot should not replace professional medical care.

Health in ChatGPT is starting to roll out to U.S. users.

You can securely connect Apple Health and supported medical records to understand your information in context, track what has changed, and have more informed conversations.https://t.co/W2E6oT8c91

— OpenAI (@OpenAI) July 23, 2026

The launch also comes under increased public attention after a lawsuit challenged the chatbot’s medical advice just one day before the announcement.

Health Data Now Follows Users Into Every Chat

ChatGPT Health was first introduced as a limited pilot through a dedicated health hub in January.

Users previously had to enter that separate section to receive responses based on their connected health information.

Introducing ChatGPT Health — a dedicated space for health conversations in ChatGPT. You can securely connect medical records and wellness apps so responses are grounded in your own health information.

Designed to help you navigate medical care, not replace it.

Join the…

— OpenAI (@OpenAI) January 7, 2026

That restriction has now been removed.

Users can choose to let ChatGPT draw insights from their connected health data throughout normal conversations, allowing it to personalise responses beyond direct medical questions.

For example, if someone asks for restaurant recommendations, ChatGPT can take dietary restrictions into account.

If a user is planning weekend activities, it can also consider information such as a recent injury when suggesting lower-impact options.

OpenAI said users can continue asking follow-up questions, provide additional context or correct outdated information during the conversation.

Connected Records Give ChatGPT More Context

The feature works by connecting data that users choose to share.

On iPhone, ChatGPT can access information from Apple Health alongside services including Function and MyFitnessPal.

Users can also connect medical records from hospital systems such as Epic and Oracle Health, as well as health platforms including One Medical and Function Health.

Health in ChatGPT is starting to roll out to U.S. users.

Open Health from the sidebar to securely connect and manage accounts, view a timeline of synced records, and explore recent data and trends.

With your permission, ChatGPT can draw on your connected medical records and… pic.twitter.com/nCNZfWAWlw

— ChatGPT (@ChatGPTapp) July 23, 2026

With access to these records, ChatGPT can compare newly uploaded laboratory results with previous tests, helping users identify changes over time while keeping all related information within a single conversation.

If you choose, ChatGPT Health lets you securely connect medical records and apps like Apple Health, MyFitnessPal, and Peloton to give personalized responses. pic.twitter.com/Nv5sA2ifrV

— OpenAI (@OpenAI) January 7, 2026

Health Questions Continue To Climb

OpenAI said testing showed that users were already discussing health topics outside the dedicated hub.

Around 70% of health-related conversations happened in standard chats rather than inside the health section.

The company also reported strong growth in usage.

Weekly health-related questions have risen from 230 million when the pilot launched in January to around 300 million today.

More than 300 million people turn to ChatGPT with health-related questions each week—and we’re continuing to improve how our models respond.

We work with hundreds of physicians around the world to measure and improve accuracy, safety, communication, context awareness,… pic.twitter.com/j8BwEkn14b

— OpenAI (@OpenAI) July 23, 2026

The rollout is reaching logged-in users in the US on the web and iOS this week.

The feature is not yet available inside OpenAI's coding tool, Codex.

OpenAI Points To Model Improvements

OpenAI said its latest models have improved their ability to answer health questions.

According to the company, GPT 5.6-Luna, the smallest model in its newest release, outperforms GPT 5.5 on HealthBench, an open-source benchmark developed by OpenAI to measure how well language models respond to health-related queries.

GPT-5.6 is a major step forward for health intelligence.

Across the lineup, we’re delivering stronger performance at lower cost: GPT-5.6 Luna outperforms GPT-5.5 at its highest reasoning setting while costing 25x less.

Together, these advances raise quality while making… pic.twitter.com/7WV4UkTiqX

— OpenAI (@OpenAI) July 10, 2026

The company also said it works with physicians to improve its health responses and reiterated that customer data connected through the feature is not used to train its AI models.

Lawsuit Puts Safety Back Into Focus

The nationwide launch comes just one day after a Florida pastor filed a lawsuit against OpenAI, claiming ChatGPT advised him not to seek medical attention for symptoms that were later linked to a life-threatening pulmonary embolism.

According to the lawsuit, the chatbot told him to trust that “God did not design your body to endlessly fail.”

The pastor claims the advice delayed urgent treatment and nearly cost him his life.

A Florida pastor has sued OpenAI in California state court alleging that ChatGPT gave him dangerous and inaccurate medical advice, often couched with spiritual guidance, causing him to delay care until he suffered a massive pulmonary embolism. https://t.co/cKhSuOAsc0

— Law360 (@Law360) July 23, 2026

OpenAI responded by pointing to its existing terms, which state that the service is “not intended for use in the diagnosis or treatment of any health condition.”

The company also told The New York Times that it is continuing to improve the safety of health and medical responses.

While promoting ChatGPT Health, OpenAI acknowledged that the system can “still make mistakes.”

The company said users should verify important information and make medical decisions with guidance from qualified healthcare professionals rather than relying solely on AI.

A Question Worth Asking Before You Trust AI

ChatGPT Health can help people organise information, explain medical terms and make health records easier to understand, but it is still designed as an assistant rather than a doctor.

As AI becomes part of more everyday health decisions, the most important question may not be what ChatGPT says, but whether users know when to seek advice from a qualified medical professional.

Using AI as a starting point can be helpful, but decisions involving symptoms, diagnosis or treatment should always be confirmed by a healthcare provider.

data.title: Robinhood CEO's X Account Hijacked To Pump Fake Memecoin That Briefly Hit $10M Valuation
data.description:
Hackers hijacked Robinhood CEO Vlad Tenev's X account on Thursday to promote a fraudulent memecoin falsely presented as part of Robinhood Chain, briefly sending the token's market capitalization to about $10 million before the scheme unraveled.

The compromised account promoted a token called Vladhood (VLAD), describing it as the "official Robinhood Chain mascot" and claiming Robinhood would eventually list the asset on its trading platform. The post included a contract address for a token created only minutes earlier, quickly attracting more than 175,000 views before users began warning that it appeared to be a scam.

⚠️ JUST IN: The threat actor who hacked the X/Twitter account of Robinhood CEO ‘Vlad’ today walked away with around 650 ETH ($1.3M)

The threat actor posted a rugpull on the account (heist took 2h , the guy walked away with 7f and all funds have been laundered) pic.twitter.com/SboFB7rFvh

— Com Feed (@thecomfeed) July 23, 2026

Robinhood later confirmed through its Robinhood Comms X account that Tenev's profile had been compromised and said it was working with X to restore access. The fraudulent post has since been removed, while Robinhood Chain's blockchain explorer labeled the token contract as a potential scam.

Fake Robinhood endorsement fueled rapid trading frenzy

The fabricated endorsement was enough to ignite a wave of speculative buying.

According to onchain data, Vladhood's market capitalization surged to roughly $10 million before falling below $5 million after Robinhood confirmed the account had been hacked. Despite the warning, the token continued trading, highlighting how onchain assets can remain active even after the social media promotion driving demand is exposed as fraudulent.

🚨 ALERT: Robinhood confirmed CEO Vlad Tenev’s X account was compromised and used to promote a fake meme coin before the post was removed. pic.twitter.com/2ZPArIJ2sJ

— Cointelegraph (@Cointelegraph) July 23, 2026

Blockchain monitor MLM estimated the attackers extracted approximately 650 Ether (ETH), worth between $1.2 million and $1.3 million, during the operation, a figure also shared by Wu Blockchain. Meanwhile, onchain investigator Jeff claimed to have identified a wallet believed to be linked to the operation after it spent just $126.81 acquiring 47.2 million VLAD tokens before the promotion.

The wallet was later estimated to be sitting on roughly $159,000 in unrealized gains, while separate onchain analysis suggested insider wallets collectively realized more than $1 million in profits during the token's rapid rise. There is currently no evidence directly linking those wallets to whoever compromised Tenev's account.

Robinhood Chain's momentum made the scam more convincing

The attack comes as Robinhood Chain has emerged as one of crypto's fastest-growing new networks following its July 1 mainnet launch.

Built as an Ethereum layer-2 network using Arbitrum technology, the blockchain was introduced to support tokenized real-world assets, decentralized finance applications and around-the-clock trading. However, memecoins have accounted for much of its early activity, with industry estimates placing cumulative decentralized exchange volume at roughly $9 billion within weeks of launch.

Hey chat. We updated our launchpad with the FASTEST Robinhood Chain TG trading bot on the market.

Keeping it free for 14 days. Take a sneek peek 👀|https://t.co/VeofkjtKHT Nock. Aim. Shoot🎯 pic.twitter.com/QHLcQoA6wA

— Nock Terminal | Robinhood Chain Launchpad (@NockTerminal) July 21, 2026

That surge in speculative trading likely amplified the effectiveness of the scam. By falsely presenting Vladhood as an official Robinhood Chain token, the attackers exploited growing interest in the ecosystem and the credibility of Tenev's verified account to lure traders into the newly created asset.

The incident also mirrors a broader pattern across the crypto industry. Binance co-founder Changpeng Zhao previously warned that hackers were increasingly targeting verified social media accounts to promote fraudulent token contract addresses, while similar attacks have previously compromised BNB Chain's X account and a WeChat account belonging to Binance co-CEO Yi He.

🚨BEWARE!

🟠 BNB Chain's official X account was just compromised. Hackers used the account to spread phishing links targeting crypto wallets.

SlowMist's chief security officer said the phishing domains behind BNB Chain’s compromised X account are tied to the notorious Inferno… pic.twitter.com/pgYBfkQx2h

— Sad Creator (@SadCreatorTalks) October 1, 2025

The Robinhood breach underscores how attackers are increasingly exploiting trusted public figures rather than exchange infrastructure itself, using social media to manufacture legitimacy and rapidly funnel speculative capital into fraudulent onchain tokens before the deception is uncovered.
